title: Homomorphism Expressivity of Spectral Invariant Graph Neural Networks

reviews.summary: The paper studies the expressive power of spectral invariant graph neural networks (GNNs) through the lens of homomorphism expressivity. Its central result (Theorem 3.3) shows that d-iteration spectral invariant GNNs are exactly characterized by the ability to distinguish graphs via homomorphism counts of a new “parallel trees” family with parallel-tree depth at most d, and proves maximality. This yields a quantitative hierarchy among spectral invariants, clarifies the impact of iterations (strictly increasing power with depth, disproving a prior conjecture), extends to higher-order spectral invariant GNNs, and provides precise subgraph counting guarantees (e.g., cycles up to length 7), with supporting experiments.
strengths: Introduces a new and elegant homomorphism characterization (parallel trees) for spectral invariant GNNs, establishing existence and maximality of their homomorphism expressivity.

Bridges two previously separate strands—spectral invariants (including projections) and homomorphism indistinguishability—via walk-encoding equivalences and tailored pebble games adapted to spectral refinement.
Resolves open questions from prior work (e.g., strictly increasing power with iterations; precise role of projections vs eigenvalues; bounding symmetric power by Local 2k-GNN).
Provides extensions to higher-order spectral invariant GNNs through a parallel strong nested ear decomposition concept.
Uses homomorphism counting benchmarks, cycle counting tasks, and a standard molecular dataset (ZINC) to qualitatively validate theoretical predictions and the proposed hierarchy.
Compares against strong baselines (MPNN, Subgraph GNN, Local 2-GNN) aligned with the theoretical framework.
Core concepts—parallel edges/trees, parallel-tree depth, and the connection to walk-encoding—are well-motivated and intuitive, aiding the reader.
Clear articulation of implications: hierarchy vs 1-WL/2-FWL, the role of iterations, and subgraph counting capabilities.
Offers a quantitative, fine-grained, and maximally tight characterization that complements and substantially extends WL-style analyses.
Establishes a reusable methodology (walk equivalence + spectral pebble game + Fürer-graph reduction) that is likely applicable to analyzing other global positional/spectral augmentations in GNNs.
Clarifies practical capabilities (e.g., which subgraphs can be counted and how many iterations suffice), informing design choices in spectral GNNs.
weaknesses: The main spectral-encoding equivalence leverages exact spectral projection information (or equivalently all walk counts up to n−1), which is computationally heavy and only approximated in practice; the gap between idealized theory and practical approximations is not discussed.

Some key steps (e.g., maximality via the spectral pebble game and Fürer-graph construction, and the higher-order “parallel strong NED” formalism) are deferred to the appendix, limiting verifiability in the main text.

Experiments validate trends but are relatively light given the breadth of claims; e.g., iteration-depth ablations are limited, and there is no explicit test showing success on C7 but failure on C8 as predicted by the theory.
Table formatting is hard to parse; the cycle-counting section focuses on 3–6 cycles though the theory highlights 7-cycles as a boundary case.
No ablation isolating eigenvalues vs. projections vs. refinement iterations to empirically substantiate the incremental gains each provides.

Some notation and figure references have minor inconsistencies or artifacts (likely due to extraction), and several crucial counterexamples are referenced but not fully visible/legible in the main body.
The higher-order section is only “informal” in the main text; readers must consult the appendix for essential definitions and proofs.

While the coverage is strong, a brief discussion of computational costs and stability in extracting spectral projections (and the implications for training/inference) would strengthen the connection to practical spectral GNN implementations.
Additional context on recent positional encodings/graph transformers that approximate global spectral structure could help position the results’ practical relevance.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive are your empirical results to approximations of projections (e.g., truncated eigenspaces, randomized SVD, top-K eigenpairs)? Can your theory be partially extended to these approximations?
2. Could you include an explicit empirical test for subgraph counting on C7 vs C8 to directly validate Corollary 3.15 and the stated iteration requirements?
3. Beyond constant initialization, how do node/edge features interact with your expressivity results? Do the characterizations extend when features are present?
4. Can you provide more details or intuition on why parallel trees (specifically) match the spectral-walk refinement dynamics and why depth corresponds to parallel-tree depth?
5. In practice, what is the computational complexity of one spectral invariant GNN layer under your formulation, and how do you recommend implementing it efficiently for medium/large graphs?
6. For the higher-order extension: could you move a concise formal definition of “parallel k-order strong NED” into the main text and give a minimal illustrative example?
7. Are there natural graph classes where spectral invariant GNNs achieve the 2-FWL bound (i.e., become equivalent), or can you prove a strict gap persists over all graph families?
reviews.assessment: This is a strong and timely theoretical paper that provides a clean, maximal homomorphism-expressivity characterization for spectral invariant GNNs and leverages it to answer several open questions, establish a clear hierarchy against other architectures, and derive concrete subgraph counting capabilities with iteration bounds. The technical approach—equating spectral projections with walk features, devising a spectral pebble game, and reducing maximality to Fürer-type constructions—is nontrivial and insightful. While experiments are supportive, they are relatively light and could better illustrate key boundaries (e.g., C7 vs C8) and the role of approximation in practice. Some essential formal details reside in the appendix, and a brief treatment of computational considerations would improve practical relevance. Overall, the contributions are original, well-motivated, and significant for the ICLR community interested in GNN expressivity; I recommend acceptance after addressing the empirical and presentation clarifications.
